era_context: Han Dynasty under Emperor Wu continues its expansionist policies. The death of Huo Qubing in 117 BCE marks a transition in Han military leadership. The Xiongnu remain a significant threat, though Han military pressure has been sustained. Rome remains in the late Republic, with ongoing factional conflicts. This is a period of relative stability in both major powers, though underlying tensions persist.

events.title: Han military reorganization and succession of military leadership
events.description: Following the death of Huo Qubing in 117 BCE, Han military leadership undergoes reorganization. General Wei Qing, Huo Qubing's uncle and the senior Han military commander, continues to lead Han forces. The year 116 BCE represents a period of consolidation and reorganization of Han military command structures. New generals and commanders are appointed to replace Huo Qubing and other deceased officers. This reorganization reflects the ongoing importance of military affairs to Emperor Wu's reign.
events.certainty_note: The general process of military reorganization is attested, but specific events for 116 BCE are not clearly distinguished in surviving sources. The year is assigned by historiographic convention.

events.title: Han expansion of administrative control in the western regions
events.description: Han administrative and military presence in the western regions continues to expand. Han garrisons and administrative posts are established or reinforced in the Tarim Basin and Xinjiang. This expansion is part of Emperor Wu's broader strategy to secure trade routes and prevent Xiongnu expansion. The establishment of these posts represents the beginning of Han territorial consolidation in Central Asia.

sources.name: Archaeological evidence from Xinjiang (pottery, inscriptions, fortifications)

events.certainty_note: The general process of Han expansion is well-attested, but precise dating of specific administrative establishments is uncertain. The period c. 116–110 BCE is assigned by historiographic consensus.

events.title: Roman political tensions and electoral disputes
events.description: Rome continues to experience political turmoil in the late Republic. Factional conflicts between the Optimates and populist movements persist. The year 116 BCE (638 AUC) sees continued electoral disputes and political maneuvering. The specific magistrates and events for this year are poorly attested in surviving sources.
events.certainty_note: Roman magistracies are documented in the Fasti, but narrative sources for this specific year are fragmentary or lost.

disconfirming_evidence: The significance of military reorganization in 116 BCE is not clearly attested in surviving sources. The year may not have been particularly significant for Han military affairs; the major campaigns and leadership changes may have occurred in adjacent years. The traditional narrative of a clear succession of military leadership from Huo Qubing to other generals may oversimplify a more complex process of institutional change.
historiographic_note: Sources for 116 BCE are similar to those for previous years. The year is not particularly well-attested in surviving sources, and much of what is known about this period is reconstructed from broader patterns of Han military and administrative activity. Archaeological evidence from Xinjiang provides supplementary information, but dating to specific years is difficult.

graph_edges.note: Military reorganization follows from Huo Qubing's death
Administrative expansion is part of broader Han military and political strategy
